content.problemStatement: There is a city where people are fond of visiting restaurants. In fact, any building in this town is either a house or a restaurant. There are N buildings in the city and they are conveniently numerated by integer numbers from 1 to N. To move from some building to others there are M two-way roads. Each road connects two buildings. It is possible that there are several roads that connect the same pair of buildings, but there won't be a road that connects a building to itself. Chef wants to make the ways to his restaurants more interesting. To do that he is going to decorate some roads. Each road has its own integer cost of decorating. Some costs may be negative. If the cost is negative Chef will get some reward for decorating this road. Now Chef doesn't have much money so he wants to decorate roads in such a way that from each house, at least one restaurant is reachable using only decorated roads. The total cost of decorated roads should be minimal. So, your task is to help him to calculate the minimal cost of any satisfactory subset. It might be negative if Chef's rewards for decorating some roads are greater than his spent money.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow. For each test case, the first line contains two integers N and M, the number of buildings and the number of roads. The second line contains a string of N characters, where 'H' represents a house and 'R' represents a restaurant. Then M lines follow, each containing three integers Xi, Yi, and Zi, describing a road that connects buildings Xi and Yi with the cost of decorating equal to Zi.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the minimal cost of any satisfactory subset of roads.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, the minimum cost is 2, which is achieved by decorating the roads between buildings 1 and 2, and between buildings 3 and 2. For the second test case, the minimum cost is 0, which is achieved by not decorating any roads. For the third test case, the minimum cost is 1, which is achieved by decorating the road between buildings 1 and 2.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a modified version of Kruskal's algorithm for finding the minimum spanning tree. We first sort all the edges by their weights. Then, we iterate over the sorted edges and add them to the minimum spanning tree if they do not form a cycle. We use a union-find data structure to keep track of the connected components. We also need to consider the case where the cost of decorating a road is negative, in which case we should always add it to the minimum spanning tree.
